items.hook: Happy to help you narrow it down without making the whole display case feel complicated.
items.bridge: At a bakery, the fastest useful split is usually bread or dessert, sweeter or lighter, and soft texture versus something more layered or flaky.
items.next_step: Ask whether they want bread or dessert first, and whether they prefer less sweet or richer flavor.
items.handoff_suggestion: Menu-guidance opportunity if the user replies with taste details.

items.hook: That is a very workable place to start.
items.bridge: With bakery desserts, sweetness level and texture usually matter more than a generic bestseller list.
items.next_step: Ask whether they want a lighter fruit direction, a richer chocolate direction, or something more bread-like and not too creamy.
items.handoff_suggestion: Good engagement signal if the user answers with flavor preference.

items.hook: Choosing the cake size is often the part that feels hardest before the details are clear.
items.bridge: For celebration cakes, the headcount and the style you want usually matter more than just picking a number at random.
items.next_step: Ask how many people are sharing, the occasion, and whether they want fruit, chocolate, or a cleaner style.
items.handoff_suggestion: Cake-selection lead if the user provides headcount and style direction.

items.hook: I can see why you want to lock this in quickly.
items.bridge: Same-day cake readiness depends a lot on the branch, the pickup time, and whether you need a simple whole cake or real decoration work.
items.next_step: Ask for store, pickup time, size, and whether writing or custom decoration matters.
items.handoff_suggestion: Send quickly to a live store or Catcher path.

items.hook: That is exactly the kind of detail worth locking down early.
items.bridge: For cake writing and candles, the exact wording, number detail, and cake size all affect whether it can be arranged cleanly.
items.next_step: Ask for the exact text, candle or topper need, and the cake size they have in mind.
items.handoff_suggestion: Customization-ready once exact wording is confirmed.

items.hook: Good call to check before making the trip.
items.bridge: Morning breads and sandwiches can move differently by branch and time because batch rhythm and rush traffic change a lot.
items.next_step: Ask which store they mean and what time they plan to arrive.

items.hook: That sounds like a real order, not casual browsing.
items.bridge: For a multi-box bakery order, the important details are usually store, date or ready time, quantity, and whether invoice or delivery is needed.
items.next_step: Ask for store, ready time, quantity, and invoice or delivery need.
items.handoff_suggestion: Route as a high-intent operational lead.

items.hook: That is a nice occasion to plan around rather than guess at.
items.bridge: Gift boxes work best when we know whether you need a shareable pastry mix, a cookie-style box, or something more celebration-focused.
items.next_step: Ask how many boxes they need, the budget range, and whether it is for gifting or for people to eat right away.
items.handoff_suggestion: Strong gifting lead if the user shares quantity or date.

items.hook: Makes sense to check that before trusting a cream or decorated cake to the trip.
items.bridge: Cake stability usually depends on type, weather, distance, and whether it is pickup or delivery, not just a simple yes or no.
items.next_step: Ask which cake they want, which store, and whether it needs delivery or self-pickup.
items.handoff_suggestion: Careful store confirmation if the request is time-sensitive or fragile.

items.hook: That is the kind of detail worth checking carefully.
items.bridge: Allergen and ingredient questions should be confirmed on the exact item instead of answered casually from memory.
items.next_step: Ask for the specific allergen or ingredient concern and the product they are asking about.

items.hook: I am glad you asked before deciding.
items.bridge: Questions like pregnancy, child suitability, or low-sugar fit depend on the exact product and what the person is trying to avoid.
items.next_step: Ask for the exact product and the concern they want checked.

items.bridge: With bakeries, people often care about different proof points such as sweetness, butter flavor, bread texture, cream feel, or how fresh something tastes.
items.next_step: Invite the user to name the exact quality point they doubt.

items.hook: Makes sense to check the offer before you place the order.
items.bridge: Bakery coupons and group-buy rules often change by store, channel, and the item category involved.
items.next_step: Ask for the store and whether they are ordering in app, in person, or by delivery.
items.handoff_suggestion: Useful bridge into a real order if the user confirms details.

items.hook: I can understand why that would feel frustrating.
items.bridge: The fastest way to check a bakery issue is usually the store, order time, and a photo if there is one.
items.next_step: Move the user to DM for order details and photos.
items.handoff_suggestion: Escalate to after-sales or store operations.

items.hook: We can keep this practical without making it vague.
items.bridge: For a larger bakery order, the price range still depends on whether you want simple breads, mixed pastries, or whole cakes.
items.next_step: Ask for quantity, intended use, and whether they care more about filling people up or gifting nicely.
items.handoff_suggestion: Budget-sensitive lead if the user answers with scope.

items.hook: Happy to move this forward quickly.
items.bridge: The safest first step is still platform DM so order details do not get scattered in public comments.
items.next_step: Ask for the store plus order details in DM.
